steps: Prepare the banana blossom by removing the tough outer purple bracts until you reach the pale heart; slice into thick wedges and soak in acidulated water to prevent browning.
Drain and rinse the young jackfruit, removing any hard core pieces, and pat dry.
In a large bowl, whisk together coconut milk, ginger-garlic paste, turmeric, chili powder, garam masala, and salt.
Toss the jackfruit pieces and banana blossom wedges in the marinade and let sit for at least 30 minutes.
Thread the marinated jackfruit and banana blossom alternately onto soaked wooden skewers.
Heat coconut oil in a grill pan over medium-high heat and sear the skewers for 4-5 minutes per side until charred and tender.
Garnish with flash-fried curry leaves and a squeeze of fresh lemon juice before serving.
Scrub the black clams thoroughly under cold water to remove any mud or grit.
Carefully pry open each clam over a bowl to catch all the dark, briny liquor; extract the meat and place it into the bowl with the juice.
Season the meat and juice with salt, pepper, garlic paste, and rocoto paste, stirring gently.
Add the finely diced red onion and minced ají limo, then pour over the fresh lime juice.
Toss in the fresh cilantro and mix well; let it marinate for no more than 2-5 minutes to keep the clams tender.
Serve immediately in small bowls, accompanied by slices of sweet potato and choclo.
Mix flour, yeast, sugar, and salt; gradually add warm water, milk, and a splash of oil to form a smooth dough. Let rise for 1.5 hours.
Grate the Sulguni and Imeruli cheeses together, mixing in one egg to bind the filling into a thick paste.
Divide the dough into balls; roll each into an oval and roll up the long sides toward the center, pinching the ends to form a boat shape.
Fill the center of the boat generously with the cheese mixture and brush the dough edges with egg wash.
Bake at 230°C (450°F) for 12-15 minutes until the crust is golden and cheese is bubbling.
Remove from oven, use a spoon to make a well in the cheese, and drop a raw egg yolk into the center.
Add a thick slice of butter and return to the oven for 1 minute (the yolk should remain runny). Serve hot to be mixed at the table.
Blend shallots, garlic, ginger, and chilies into a smooth spice paste (bumbu).
In a heavy-bottomed pot, combine coconut milk, spice paste, lemongrass, galangal, and turmeric leaf over medium heat.
Bring to a boil, then add the beef and lower heat to a simmer, stirring occasionally.
Once the coconut milk thickens and turns oily (kalio stage), add the toasted coconut (kerisik) and salt.
Continue cooking on very low heat, stirring constantly, until the liquid evaporates and the spices dark brown and cling to the meat.
Serve at room temperature; it’s best eaten the following day.
Remove seeds from chiles and fry the seeds in a pan until completely charred and black; soak the seeds in water to remove bitterness.
Toast the chiles lightly, then soak in hot water until soft; blend with the charred seeds.
Sauté onions, garlic, raisins, almonds, and plantains in lard until golden; blend with spices and bread to create a thick paste.
Fry the combined pastes in a large pot with lard until fragrant and darkened.
Slowly whisk in chicken stock and the chocolate, simmering until the sauce reaches a velvet-thick consistency.
Serve over poached turkey or chicken with a garnish of sesame seeds.
Grind shallots, garlic, ginger, turmeric, and chilies into a paste; crush the andaliman pepper separately.
Sauté the spice paste with lemongrass and lime leaves until aromatic.
Add the pork cubes and cook until the meat is browned and juices are released.
Add a small amount of water and simmer until the pork is tender and the liquid has reduced by half.
Lower the heat and slowly pour in the blood, stirring constantly to prevent curdling until the sauce turns dark and thickens.
Finish with crushed andaliman pepper for the signature numbing citrus finish.
Blanch grape leaves in boiling water for 2 minutes if fresh, or rinse thoroughly if brined.
In a bowl, combine ground beef, rice, onions, chopped herbs, tomato paste, salt, and pepper; mix by hand until tacky.
Place a leaf flat, add a tablespoon of filling at the base, and roll tightly into a small cylinder, tucking the sides in.
Line the bottom of a heavy pot with torn leaves and pack the tolma in tight, circular layers.
Add enough water or light broth to barely cover them; weight them down with an inverted plate to prevent unrolling.
Simmer on low heat for 45-50 minutes until the rice is tender and water is absorbed.
Serve hot with a side of matzoon mixed with minced garlic.
Simmer beef shank with star anise, cassia, ginger, and peppercorns for 3 hours; strain to keep the broth crystal clear.
Mix flour with water and alkaline solution (peng hui); knead until the gluten is highly elastic and the dough doesn't snap when stretched.
Pull and fold the dough repeatedly into thin strands (or slice into thin ribbons if hand-pulling is too technical).
Boil the fresh noodles for only 1-2 minutes until they float.
Parboil the radish slices in the beef broth until translucent.
Assemble by placing noodles in a bowl, topping with thin slices of the cooked beef and radish.
Ladle the hot, clear broth over the top and garnish heavily with cilantro, scallions, and a signature swirl of deep red chili oil.
Make a 'rof' (herb paste) by pounding parsley, garlic, and chili; poke holes in the fish and stuff it with this paste.
Sear the fish in oil until golden, then remove and set aside.
Fry onions and tomato paste in the same oil until the oil separates; add the fermented fish (guédj) for deep umami.
Add water, cassava, eggplant, and carrots; simmer until vegetables are tender, then remove them and set aside.
Wash the broken rice and steam it over the bubbling broth for 15 minutes.
Add the pre-steamed rice into the broth, ensuring the liquid level is just right to absorb without burning.
Arrange the fish and vegetables back on top of the rice for the final 10 minutes of steaming. Serve on a large communal platter.
Boil the lamb chunks with cardamom and bay leaves, skimming the foam until the meat is halfway cooked; drain and set the meat aside.
Slowly whisk the liquefied jameed into a large pot and bring to a gentle simmer, stirring constantly in one direction to prevent curdling.
Add the lamb to the jameed sauce and simmer on low for 1-2 hours until the meat is 'falling off the bone' tender.
Cook the rice with ghee and turmeric to give it a vibrant yellow color and rich aroma.
To assemble, layer a large platter with shrak bread and ladle a small amount of jameed sauce to soak it.
Mound the rice over the bread, arrange the lamb pieces on top, and pour more sauce over the entire dish.
Garnish heavily with toasted nuts and parsley. Serve the remaining sauce in a bowl on the side to be poured by guests.
Pat the cuy completely dry; this is the most important step for achieving crispy skin.
Create a marinade by mixing huacatay, garlic, cumin, ají amarillo, and chicha de jora.
Rub the marinade inside the cavity and all over the skin; let it rest in the fridge for at least 4 hours (uncovered to dry the skin).
Traditionally roasted on a spit, but for home use: place on a wire rack over a baking tray.
Roast at 180°C (350°F) for 45 minutes, then increase to 220°C (425°F) for 10 minutes to blister the skin.
Serve whole or halved, accompanied by 'papa helada' (boiled potatoes) and an onion salsa (sarsa criolla).
Thaw the pure açaí pulp slightly until it reaches a thick, creamy, pudding-like consistency.
Pour the deep purple cream into a clay bowl; do not blend with bananas or juice.
Prepare the fried fish separately, seasoned simply with salt and lime.
Serve the bowl of açaí alongside the hot fish.
The diner sprinkles the crunchy manioc flour over the açaí with each spoonful, alternating bites with the salty fish.
If desired, a very small amount of sugar can be sprinkled on top to balance the earthy bitterness.

ingredients.name: carrots (mix of yellow and orange, julienned)

steps: Heat the fat in a heavy cast-iron Kazan until smoking; sear the meat until deeply browned and remove.
Fry the onions until golden brown, then add the carrots and cook until softened; return the meat to the pot.
Add water to barely cover, add spices, barberries, and chickpeas; simmer for 40 minutes to create the 'Zirvak' (flavor base).
Place whole garlic bulbs into the liquid, then layer the washed rice evenly over the top—do not stir.
Add boiling water over a slotted spoon so as not to disturb the rice layer, until it's 2cm above the rice.
Cook on high until water evaporates, then poke holes to the bottom for steam, cover tightly, and steam on very low for 20 minutes.
Remove garlic, mix the layers thoroughly, and serve on a large 'Lagan' platter.
Whisk rice flour, turmeric, coconut milk, and sparkling water (for extra bubbles) into a thin batter; let rest for 30 minutes.
Heat a large wok or skillet with oil; sauté a few slices of pork and shrimp until just cooked.
Pour a thin layer of batter into the pan, swirling quickly to coat the edges; it should make a loud 'hissing' sound.
Place a handful of bean sprouts on one half, cover with a lid for 2 minutes to steam the sprouts.
Remove lid and cook for another 2 minutes until the edges are crispy and start to lift away from the pan.
Fold the crepe in half and slide onto a plate.
To eat: tear a piece of the crepe, wrap it inside a large mustard or lettuce leaf with fresh herbs, and dip in nuoc cham.
Slow-cook the minced onions in a dry pot over low heat for 45 minutes, stirring constantly until they lose all moisture and turn into a jam-like paste.
Add the niter kibbeh and let it melt into the onions; then stir in the berbere spice and cook for another 15 minutes.
Add the garlic-ginger paste and a splash of wine or broth if the mixture is too dry; the base should be deep mahogany.
Add the chicken drumsticks, coating them thoroughly in the onion paste; cover and simmer for 45 minutes until tender.
Make small incisions in the hard-boiled eggs and add them to the pot for the last 10 minutes to soak up the sauce.
Finish with a squeeze of lemon juice.
Serve on a large piece of injera; use the bread as your only utensil to scoop up the chicken and sauce.
In a tall glass, layer the bottom with sweetened beans, macapuno, kaong, and other preserved fruits.
Fill the glass with finely shaved ice until it reaches the brim.
Pour the evaporated milk generously over the ice, letting it seep down to the bottom layers.
Top the ice with a scoop of ube halaya and a square of leche flan.
Place a final scoop of ube ice cream on top and sprinkle with crunchy pinipig.
Serve with a long spoon; the diner must vigorously mix everything together before eating.
Thread the lamb sweetbreads onto a horizontal skewer.
Carefully wrap the cleaned intestines around the sweetbreads tightly to form a thick log (spit).
Roast over a charcoal fire (or a high-heat grill), rotating constantly until the exterior is deeply browned and crispy.
Slice a portion of the meat off the log and place it on a flat griddle.
Chop the meat finely with spatulas on the heat, mixing in the diced tomatoes and peppers.
Season aggressively with oregano, pul biber, cumin, and salt.
Slice the bread open, press it onto the meat to soak up the fats, then fill with the chopped mixture. Serve hot.
Mash the boiled potatoes while hot and mix with the twaróg until smooth; season heavily with black pepper.
Sauté half the onions in butter until golden brown and stir them into the potato-cheese filling.
Knead flour and warm water into a soft, elastic dough; let it rest under a warm bowl for 30 minutes.
Roll dough thin and cut circles using a glass; place a spoonful of filling in the center and pinch edges to seal.
Boil in salted water until they float, then cook for 2 more minutes.
Sauté the remaining onions in plenty of butter until caramelized; toss the boiled pierogi in the onion butter to brown slightly.
Serve with a side of cold sour cream.
Heat oil and sauté star anise, cinnamon stick, and curry leaves; add onions and ginger-garlic paste until golden.
Add the Durban curry powder and tomatoes, cooking until the oil begins to separate from the spice base.
Add the meat and brown thoroughly; then add potatoes and enough water to create a thick, rich gravy.
Simmer for 1 hour until the meat is tender and the potatoes have slightly thickened the sauce.
Cut the loaf of bread in half (or quarters) and hollow out the center, keeping the bread 'plug' intact.
Ladle the hot curry into the bread cavity, letting the gravy soak into the walls of the loaf.
Place the 'plug' back on top and serve with a side of carrot salad (sambal).
Fry the curry paste in a thick layer of coconut cream until the oil splits and becomes fragrant.
Add chicken thighs and remaining coconut milk; simmer gently for 45 minutes until the meat is tender.
Season the broth with palm sugar and fish sauce to balance the spicy, salty, and sweet notes.
Boil 3/4 of the egg noodles until soft and set aside.
Deep-fry the remaining 1/4 of the noodles until they are golden and extremely crispy.
Place the soft noodles in a bowl, ladle over the chicken and plenty of broth.
Top with the crispy noodles and serve with a side plate of pickled mustard greens, raw shallots, and lime.
Marinate the eel slices in white wine, salt, and pepper for at least 30 minutes.
In a large pot, sauté the onions, garlic, carrots, and tomatoes until soft.
Add the paprika and oregano, then pour in the fish stock and the marinade from the eel.
Add the potatoes and simmer for 15 minutes until they begin to soften.
Gently place the eel slices into the pot. Simmer for another 10-12 minutes until the fish is opaque and firm.
Just before serving, stir in the heavy cream and remove from heat.
Serve in heated clay bowls, garnished with fresh cilantro.

ingredients.name: Goat meat (with bone, cut into pieces)

steps: Marinate the goat meat in cumin, garlic, salt, and half of the Chicha de Jora for at least 2 hours.
In a large heavy-bottomed pot, heat the achiote oil and brown the goat meat in batches. Remove and set aside.
In the same pot, sauté the onions, bell pepper, and tomatoes until soft and fragrant.
Return the meat to the pot and add the remaining Chicha de Jora, Naranjilla juice, and panela.
Bring to a boil, then reduce heat to a low simmer. Cover and cook for 2 to 2.5 hours until the meat is falling off the bone.
Uncover for the last 20 minutes to allow the sauce to reduce into a thick, glossy consistency.
Stir in the fresh cilantro just before serving with yellow rice and maduros (fried ripe plantains).
Soak the salt beef in cold water overnight in the refrigerator to remove excess salt. Rinse before cooking.
Place the yellow split peas into a cotton 'pudding bag' or several layers of cheesecloth, tying it securely but leaving room for the peas to expand.
Place the salt beef and the pea bag into a large stockpot. Cover with fresh water and bring to a boil.
Reduce heat and simmer for about 1.5 to 2 hours until the beef begins to soften.
Add the turnip, carrots, and cabbage wedges to the pot. Simmer for 20 minutes.
Add the potatoes and continue simmering for another 20-25 minutes until all vegetables are fork-tender.
Remove the pea bag. Empty the peas into a bowl, add butter and black pepper, and mash until smooth to create the pease pudding.
Serve the meat and vegetables on a large platter with the pease pudding on the side.

ingredients.name: Reindeer fat or beef tallow (finely shredded)

steps: Place the shredded fat in a large bowl and cream it by hand (the heat from your hand is traditional for achieving the right texture) until it becomes light and fluffy.
Slowly add the seal oil or vegetable oil, a little at a time, while continuing to whip the mixture vigorously.
Add a small amount of water or snow to the mixture to increase the volume and achieve a white, creamy consistency.
If using sugar, fold it in slowly until dissolved.
Gently fold in the wild berries by hand so they remain whole and do not bleed too much color into the white base.
Chill the mixture in a cold area or freezer until firm but scoopable.
Serve immediately as a high-energy treat or side dish.

ingredients.name: Shortening or marrow fat (for greasing stone)

steps: Sift the blue cornmeal into a large traditional clay or mixing bowl.
Dissolve the juniper ash in cold water and strain through a fine cloth into the cornmeal to remove any grit.
Gradually add the boiling water to the cornmeal mixture, stirring constantly until it forms a smooth, thin, pourable batter with no lumps.
Heat the Piki stone (sandstone slab) over a steady fire until a drop of water sizzles and dances on the surface (approx. 350°F).
Lightly grease the stone with a small amount of fat or oil using a clean cloth.
Using your fingers or a flat scoop, quickly smear a very thin layer of batter across the hot stone in a single, fluid motion.
Wait seconds for the edges to curl and the bread to turn translucent. Gently peel the sheet away from the stone.
While still hot and pliable, place the sheet on a flat surface and roll it tightly into a scroll. Repeat the process, often layering two sheets together before rolling.
Let the rolls cool until crisp and brittle.
